primary_diagnosis.name: Acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage from an NSAID-induced bleeding peptic ulcer, with hypovolaemic (Class III haemorrhagic) shock

pathophysiology_summary: Chronic high-dose non-selective NSAID use (naproxen 500 mg BD) inhibits COX-1, depleting protective gastric prostaglandins and eroding the mucosa to produce a peptic ulcer that has bled into the upper GI tract. Digested blood presents as coffee-ground haematemesis and melaena (bacterial/acid degradation of haemoglobin), localising the source proximal to the ligament of Treitz. The volume of blood loss (repeated haematemesis plus several large melaena stools) has produced Class III hypovolaemic shock: compensatory tachycardia (HR 118), a narrowed/borderline-low supine BP (95/60), postural pre-syncope, peripheral vasoconstriction (pallor, cold, delayed capillary refill) and early cerebral hypoperfusion (drowsiness). This is a time-critical, potentially fatal haemorrhage requiring emergency resuscitation, transfusion and urgent endoscopic haemostasis.

differential.diagnosis: Acute upper gastrointestinal haemorrhage (bleeding peptic ulcer)
differential.evidence_basis: Coffee-ground haematemesis plus melaena with haemodynamic instability is a classic upper GI bleed picture.
Considered for any GI bleed, but coffee-ground haematemesis localises the source to the upper tract.

differential.diagnosis: Benign stool/vomit discolouration (e.g. oral iron, bismuth, dark foods)
differential.evidence_basis: Can mimic melaena, but does not cause haematemesis, tachycardia or shock.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening carer report alone the AI Doctor should already rank a significant GI bleed with possible shock at the top and begin escalation-oriented questioning, not reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: NSAID-induced bleeding peptic ulcer (upper GI haemorrhage)
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic high-dose naproxen supplies a strong mechanism; coffee-ground haematemesis + melaena + postural pre-syncope fit a large upper GI bleed.
Another cause of massive upper GI bleeding; no chronic liver disease history offered here, but management is the same emergency pathway.
Coffee-ground haematemesis localises the source proximal to the ligament of Treitz.
Digested blood (melaena/coffee grounds) rather than watery diarrhoea, and no fever — not an infective picture.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The NSAID history strengthens peptic ulcer as the mechanism; variceal bleeding remains an important-not-to-miss alternative but does not change the emergency triage.

differential.diagnosis: Acute upper GI haemorrhage from an NSAID-induced bleeding peptic ulcer with hypovolaemic shock
differential.evidence_basis: Endoscopy (sealed) would confirm an actively bleeding gastric/duodenal ulcer and bloods an acute haemoglobin drop; the telehealth-appropriate conclusion is a presumptive massive upper GI bleed with shock requiring immediate emergency transfer.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive confirmation (endoscopy, serial haemoglobin) is inpatient-only. The AI Doctor cannot diagnose the exact ulcer remotely and must escalate on the clinical picture.

red_flags.description: Haematemesis (coffee-ground) with melaena — active gastrointestinal haemorrhage
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms significant upper GI blood loss localised proximal to the ligament of Treitz.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to recognise active bleeding leads to under-triage and fatal exsanguination.

red_flags.description: Postural pre-syncope, tachycardia (HR 118) and borderline-low supine BP (95/60) — haemodynamic instability / hypovolaemic shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signs of Class III haemorrhagic shock; indicates large-volume blood loss and need for immediate resuscitation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delay causes progression to decompensated shock, cardiac arrest and death.

red_flags.description: Pallor, diaphoresis, cold peripheries and drowsiness — peripheral shutdown with early cerebral hypoperfusion
red_flags.clinical_significance: End-organ hypoperfusion; a sign the patient is decompensating and cannot wait for routine review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Altered conscious state can progress to collapse and aspiration of vomited blood.

red_flags.description: Chronic high-dose NSAID (naproxen) — ongoing mucosal injury and antiplatelet effect worsening the bleed
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies the causative agent that must be ceased immediately and never restarted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued NSAID administration perpetuates the haemorrhage and risks recurrent catastrophic bleeding.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: A supine BP of 95/60 can look only mildly low; in an older adult on shock's compensatory tachycardia it signals major blood loss. Being reassured by the single supine number and deferring to a next-day facility doctor is the fatal error the source note explicitly warns against.
Attributing black stool to a lower GI source or to diet/iron, missing that coffee-ground haematemesis localises it to the upper tract.
Dismissing melaena as iron-supplement or food discolouration and ignoring the concurrent haematemesis and shock.

Attempting to manage at the facility (oral fluids, watchful waiting, arranging bloods) instead of activating an emergency ambulance — the definitive treatment (transfusion + endoscopic haemostasis) is hospital-only.

risk_scores.tool_name: Glasgow-Blatchford Score (pre-endoscopy risk of upper GI bleed)
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: High (markedly elevated) — driven by haemodynamic instability, melaena, syncope
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A high score mandates urgent admission and endoscopy; effectively no low-risk pathway exists here.
Tachycardia, narrowed pulse pressure, postural pre-syncope and altered mentation indicate ~30-40% blood-volume loss requiring immediate resuscitation and transfusion.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Rectal examination confirming melaena on the glove
Capillary refill time (delayed to 4 seconds) and the weak, thready pulse quality
True degree of postural blood-pressure drop (postural testing is unsafe here)
Serum haemoglobin and the acute severe drop confirming blood loss
Upper endoscopy identifying an actively bleeding gastric or duodenal ulcer
Objective assessment of conscious level beyond the carer's 'drowsy' report
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Immediate 000 call for a Priority 1 emergency ambulance; instruct facility staff to lay the patient flat with legs elevated, keep him strictly nil by mouth, cease the naproxen, and roll him into the lateral (recovery) position if he vomits, while staying with him and monitoring until paramedics arrive.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the presence of a serious GI bleed (the constellation is unambiguous); the real risk is UNDER-action — mislabelling it as stable, arranging non-urgent bloods, or deferring to a next-day review instead of an emergency ambulance.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NICE NG12 / CG141 — Acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Resuscitate haemodynamically unstable patients and arrange urgent (immediate for unstable) endoscopy; risk-stratify with Glasgow-Blatchford; stop causative NSAIDs.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Australian Resuscitation Council — recognition of haemorrhagic shock
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Tachycardia with postural symptoms and peripheral shutdown indicates significant volume loss; activate emergency services and position supine with legs raised.
